sources.notes: Statement and constraints: count substrings of a binary string where the first player wins a circular adjacent-pair removal game.
Official editorial code maps 0 to +1 and 1 to -3, then counts prefix pairs by index modulo 4 with four inequality thresholds.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial code was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Count substrings for which the first player wins a game where the first player may remove only 00 and the second player may remove any adjacent pair containing at least one 1.
annotation.constraints: n is up to 3e5, so after deriving a constant-time substring criterion the counting must be O(n log n) or better.
annotation.core_idea: Game analysis reduces a substring to a count criterion depending only on its length modulo 4 and on score = zeros - 3 * ones. For prefix score p[i], a substring (j, i] is winning iff p[i] - p[j] is greater than one of four thresholds determined by (i - j) mod 4. Maintain previous prefix scores in four residue classes of j mod 4 and query how many are below the current bound.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time with four segment trees or Fenwick/order-statistic structures, O(n) memory.
tricks: Convert the game outcome into an inequality on zeros - 3*ones.
Group prefix indices by residue modulo 4 because the winning threshold depends on substring length modulo 4.
Count prefix pairs satisfying a strict inequality with range-count data structures.

solution_variants.name: prefix-score-residue-counting
solution_variants.summary: Build prefix score p using +1 for zero and -3 for one. For each right endpoint, query four data structures for previous prefixes in each residue class whose score is less than p[i] minus the residue-specific threshold, then insert p[i] into its residue class.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The first step is characterizing optimal play in the adjacent-pair removal game as a numeric winning criterion.
The substring criterion is counted by transforming zeros and ones into a prefix score p[i].
The official solution uses four segment trees to dynamically count previous prefix scores below a bound.
Previous prefixes are partitioned by index modulo 4 because each residue gives a different threshold.
tags.description: Count pairs, transitions, or prefix states after grouping indices or states by residue modulo a small fixed modulus.
tags.created_reason: Existing residue-class-decomposition covers splitting independent chains; this problem needs the more specific prefix-pair counting pattern by residue class.

tags.evidence: A substring is counted exactly when its transformed prefix-score difference exceeds a residue-specific threshold.
